steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the function of the references to 'population density' and 'individual physical fitness' in the text as a whole. The passage says some studies emphasize population density, others physical fitness — but walking decisions actually depend on 'multiple conditions and needs.' These two factors are examples of what individual studies emphasize, set against the larger reality that many factors matter.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'They represent factors that have been identified as important influences on walking decisions but that the text suggests are merely some of the many factors that may contribute to people's decision-making about walking.': Exactly right — studies identify them as important, but the passage (via Alfonzo) argues they are just two of many factors. ✓
  • Choice B — 'They are examples of factors that studies suggest are important but that the text claims most people rarely consider when making walking decisions.': The text never claims people ignore these factors when walking — it argues that researchers' focus on isolated factors is too narrow. ✗
  • Choice C — 'They identify factors that Alfonzo argues have been overemphasized but that the text asserts are relevant to most people's walking decisions.': Alfonzo's point is that our understanding is insufficiently robust because studies isolate single factors — she's not endorsing these factors' relevance while calling them overemphasized. ✗
  • Choice D — 'They illustrate factors that researchers believe people consider in most contexts but that the text argues are unique to walking decisions made by people in Boston.': The text never limits these factors to Boston — Boston is mentioned only as a city with high pedestrian traffic, not as the context for these studies. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
